summary: Guan Yu; the fall begins. Xu Huang's relief column shatters his army at Fancheng - Xu Huang fights Guan Yu personally; the wounded shoulder slows him; Guan Ping pulls him out. Meanwhile Lu Meng has crossed the river in white-robed merchant disguise; Jingzhou falls without a fight. Guan Yu retreats south to find his cities all flying Wu flags. Maicheng next.

Xu Huang; Cao Cao's chosen relief commander. Crosses the floodwaters with a fresh army; engages Guan Yu's perimeter at Fan; duels Guan Yu personally (old friend on the other side of the line); breaks the siege. The Wei side's redemption from the Yu Jin disaster.
Lu Xun; with Lu Meng directs the Jingzhou takeover; sends conciliatory letters keeping Mi Fang and Fu Shiren tractable.
Cao Cao; watches the trap close - coordinates with Sun Quan; sends Xu Huang to relieve Fan.
Cao Ren; finally relieved at Fancheng by Xu Huang's column.
Guan Ping; with Guan Yu through Xu Huang's attack; helps pull the wounded Guan Yu off the battlefield.
Liao Hua; rides out from the encircled army to seek help; gets through to Liu Feng and Meng Da at Shangyong who refuse to come.
Liu Feng; at Shangyong; refuses to send relief to Guan Yu - the cousin's fatal abandonment that Liu Bei will avenge later.
Meng Da; counsels Liu Feng against helping Guan Yu - protect your own command; the betrayal that will see Meng Da defect to Wei.
Meng Da (duplicate record).
Mi Fang; in Jingzhou - surrenders Gong'an to Lu Meng without a fight; Mi Zhu's brother turns coat.
Ma Liang; bears Guan Yu's despatches to Chengdu.
Wang Fu; with Guan Yu in the retreat; warned him about Lu Meng's deception.
Zhao Lei; with Guan Yu's force in the Jingzhou disaster.
Sun Quan; coordinates Lu Meng's takeover; ready to receive the prisoner.
Zhuge Jin; sent again as Wu envoy.
Zhuge Liang; in Chengdu; the news of Jingzhou's loss reaches him.
Ding Feng; with the Wu pursuit force.
Ding Feng (duplicate record).
Dong Zhao; on Cao Cao's council.
Han Dang; with the Wu army.
Jiang Qin; with Lu Meng's force taking Jingzhou.
Liang Da; minor figure.
Lu Fan; with Wu's pursuit force.
Lu Fan (duplicate record).
Lu Jing; on Sun Quan's council.
Xiahou Shang; with Cao Cao.
Xu Shang; with Xu Huang's column.
Xu Sheng; with the Wu pursuit force.
Yi Ji; in Liu Bei's court.
Zhou Tai; with the Wu pursuit force.
Zhou Tai (duplicate record).

Guan Yu dies. Trapped at Maicheng with a handful of men; tries to break out at night toward Xichuan; ambushed at Linju by Ma Zhong; lassoed off his horse with a hook-rope; captured with Guan Ping. Refuses to surrender to Sun Quan; beheaded together with his son. The body lies in Linju; the spirit goes on. The novel's most cinematic death sequence: Guan Yu's ghost roars through the clouds 'Return my head!' over Pu Jing's monastery; haunts Sun Quan's banquet hall and possesses Lu Meng to kill him on the spot in revenge.
Guan Ping; captured with his father at Linju; refuses to surrender; beheaded with Guan Yu. The father-son death together.
Ma Zhong; the Wu officer who throws the hook-rope that takes Guan Yu off his horse - the captor of the god.
Ma Zhong (duplicate record).
Pan Zhang; commands the Linju ambush that takes Guan Yu; receives Guan Yu's Green Dragon saber. Will be killed by Guan Xing in revenge at Yiling.

Sun Quan; interrogates Guan Yu after capture; tries to win him over; on Zuo Xian's advice has him executed. Then haunted by Guan Yu's ghost at the victory banquet - Lu Meng possessed and dying screaming at his table; horrified Sun Quan ships Guan Yu's head to Cao Cao.
Wang Fu; with Guan Yu at Maicheng; later dashes himself off the wall to die when news of Guan Yu's execution reaches him.
Zhao Lei; with Guan Yu at Maicheng; killed in the breakout.

Cao Cao; Sun Quan ships him Guan Yu's head in a box, hoping to shift Shu's vengeance onto Wei. Cao Cao buries the head with full noble honours - a sandalwood body for the head; a marquis's funeral. The famous gesture; also: Cao Cao falls ill thereafter and never recovers.

Liu Bei; in Chengdu - has the recurring dream of Guan Yu's bloody apparition before any news arrives. Then the official report. Vomits blood, faints. The oath of vengeance against Wu that produces the Yiling campaign.
Zhuge Liang; receives Liu Bei's mourning; tries to talk him out of the immediate revenge expedition.
Zhang Fei; receives the news at Langzhong; weeps blood; vows revenge.
Zhao Yun; in Chengdu when the news comes.
Ma Chao; in Liu Bei's court when the news comes.
Huang Zhong; with the Shu army when the news comes.
Fa Zheng; in Liu Bei's court.
Guan Xing; receives the news of his father's death; sworn to vengeance - will play that out at Yiling.
Liao Hua; arrives in Chengdu to report Liu Feng and Meng Da's refusal to relieve Guan Yu.
Liu Feng; named in Liao Hua's accusation; will be executed in chapter 79 for the refusal.
Meng Da; named in Liao Hua's accusation; will defect to Wei before he can be punished.
Meng Da (duplicate record).
Ma Liang; with the Shu court.
Lu Fan; sent by Sun Quan to negotiate; later named in the Wu post-Guan Yu council.
Lu Fan (duplicate record).
Lu Su; named in passing - already dead; in the recap of the broken alliance.
Sima Yi; advises Cao Cao to bury Guan Yu's head honourably so the vengeance goes to Wu, not Wei. The classic deflection move.
Zhang Zhao; advises Sun Quan to ship the head to Cao Cao.
Zhang Zhao (duplicate record).
Yu Jin; the disgraced prisoner; sent back to Wei now that the deal with Sun Quan is made.
Zhou Yu; named in passing - already dead; in recap.
Zhu Ran; with the Wu force at Linju.
Liu Li; named in passing in Liu Bei's court.
Liu Mao; named in passing.
Liu Mao (duplicate record).
Liu Yan; minor figure named in passing.
Liu Yan (duplicate record).
Liu Yan (duplicate record).
Liu Yan (duplicate record).
Shentu Pan; minor figure named in passing.
Wu Yi; named in passing in Liu Bei's court.
Yan Liang; named in passing - already long dead.
Wen Chou; named in passing - already long dead.
Xu Jing; in Liu Bei's court.
Yi Ji; in Liu Bei's court.
Lady Sun; named in passing - recap of Liu Bei's old marriage to her.
Lady Sun (duplicate record).

Hua Tuo; summoned to treat Cao Cao's head-pain. Proposes opening the skull with an axe to remove the source - Cao Cao thinks he means to murder him in revenge for Guan Yu; throws Hua Tuo in prison where he dies of starvation. With him dies his Book of the Black Bag - the jailer's wife burns the manuscript while it is being copied by a grateful jailer. Chinese medicine's great loss.

Cao Cao dies. Haunted by Guan Yu's ghost in dreams (and: voices in the Jianshi Hall, ghostly figures of his murdered victims - Empress Fu, Consort Dong, Dong Cheng, Ma Teng). Has the cypress tree from Yuelu cut down and bleeds; the head-pain redoubles. After killing Hua Tuo, lingering, gives his deathbed instructions to Cao Pi and the court - 72 decoy tombs to be built; the famous 'concubines distribute my fragrant possessions.' Dies at 66.
Cao Pi; receives Cao Cao's dying instructions; succeeds him as King of Wei. Will depose the Han emperor before the end of the next chapter.
Cao Zhang; Cao Cao's middle son; named - comes to mourn but the succession is already settled.
Cao Zhi; the literary son; in disgrace from Cao Cao's last years; will be tested by Cao Pi next chapter with the seven-pace poem.
Lady Bian; Cao Cao's chief consort; mother of Cao Pi, Cao Zhang, Cao Zhi; named in attendance at the deathbed.
Liu Bei; off-stage; in Chengdu still mourning Guan Yu.
Guan Yu; appears as a vengeful ghost in Cao Cao's dreams - drives him to the final illness.
Sima Yi; senior on Cao Cao's deathbed council; positioned as the steady hand under Cao Pi.
Chen Qun; senior counsel at the Wei deathbed.
Chen Qun (duplicate record).
Chen Jiao; senior counsel at the deathbed.
Chen Jiao (duplicate record).
Jia Xu; senior counsel at the deathbed.
Jia Xu (duplicate record).
Xiahou Dun; senior on Cao Cao's deathbed council; will die soon.
Cao Hong; with the Cao clan at the transition.
Cao Hong (duplicate record).
Cao Ang; named in passing - Cao Cao's long-dead eldest son who died at Wancheng.
Cao Xiong; Cao Cao's son; named in passing.
Chen Deng; named in passing - historical recap.
Consort Dong; named in passing in Cao Cao's haunted dreams - his murdered victim.
Dong Cheng; named in passing - appears as a ghost in Cao Cao's deathbed visions.
Fu Wan; named in passing - Empress Fu's father whom Cao Cao killed; appears in the ghost visions.
Hua Xin; in the Wei court at the transition.
Lady Liu; named in passing.
Lady Liu (duplicate record).
Liang Da; minor figure named in passing.
Ma Teng; named in passing - appears in Cao Cao's deathbed ghost visions.
Sima Fu; senior Wei figure at the transition.
Su Yue; minor figure named in passing.
Sun Quan; off-stage; the Wei transition reaches Wu by despatch.
Yuan Shao; named in passing - appears in Cao Cao's ghost visions.
Yuan Shao (duplicate record).
Zhou Tai; named in passing on the Wu side.
Zhou Tai (duplicate record).
Zhuge Liang; off-stage in Chengdu.
Guan Xing; in Liu Bei's court mourning Guan Yu.

Cao Pi; consolidates power. Tests Cao Zhi with the famous seven-pace-poem ordeal - Cao Zhi must compose a poem in seven paces or die. He produces 'beans burnt under the bean-stalk fire' as a parable of brotherly war; Cao Pi spares him but demotes him. Then executes Ding Yi for backing Cao Zhi. Also receives Meng Da's defection from Shu.

Cao Zhi; the chapter's tragic centerpiece. Composes the seven-pace poem ('Cooking beans by burning bean-stalks / The beans inside the pot do weep / Born of the same root we two / Why must we hurt each other so?'). Lives; demoted and exiled. The poem becomes a Chinese-literature landmark.
Ding Yi; Cao Zhi's chief partisan; executed by Cao Pi after the seven-pace ordeal.
Lady Bian; pleads with Cao Pi to spare Cao Zhi; her intercession is partly why he gets the seven-pace test instead of immediate execution.
Cao Zhang; brings troops to the capital after Cao Cao's death - Cao Pi anxious; he turns out to have come to mourn, not contest.
Cao Xiong; Cao Cao's son; commits suicide rather than answer Cao Pi's summons.
Liu Bei; off-stage; the Liu Feng/Meng Da reckoning reaches him.

Meng Da; defects to Wei from Shangyong; warns Cao Pi that Liu Feng is about to attack; sends Liu Feng the famous insulting letter advising him to defect too - Liu Feng's refusal makes the battle inevitable. Cao Pi gives Meng Da Xincheng as his command.
Meng Da (duplicate record).

Liu Feng; refuses Meng Da's defection offer; battles Meng Da and the Wei relief column led by Xiahou Shang and Xu Huang; loses Shangyong; flees to Chengdu - Liu Bei executes him for the refusal to relieve Guan Yu.
Xiahou Shang; leads Wei's relief column against Liu Feng at Shangyong.
Xu Huang; with the Wei column at Shangyong.
Liu Ye; on Cao Pi's council.
Jia Kui; in the Wei court at the transition.
Jia Xu; senior Wei counsel.
Jia Xu (duplicate record).
Chen Qun; senior Wei counsel.
Chen Qun (duplicate record).
Chen Jiao; senior Wei counsel.
Chen Jiao (duplicate record).
Hua Xin; senior Wei counsel; will be the one drafting the abdication edict in chapter 80.
Huan Jie; senior Wei counsel.
Wang Lang; senior Wei counsel.
Wang Lang (duplicate record).
Xin Pi; senior Wei counsel.
Xiahou Dun; named in passing - dies in this chapter, very brief.
Xu Chu; with Cao Pi.
Guan Yu; in the recap of his death and the political fallout.
Liao Hua; arrives in Chengdu reporting Meng Da's defection and Liu Feng's loss of Shangyong.
Ma Chao; in Liu Bei's court.
Pang De; named in passing in the Wei dead-honours recap.
Peng Yang; in Liu Bei's Shu - conspires with Meng Da; exposed and executed.
Shen Dan; defects from Shu to Wei with Meng Da.
Shen Yi; Shen Dan's brother; defects to Wei with him.
Lü Ju; minor Wu officer named in passing.
Sun Quan; off-stage; receives news of the Wei transition.
Xu Zhi; minor figure named in passing.
Xu Zhi (duplicate record).
Xu Zhi (duplicate record).
Yu Jin; the disgraced surrenderer; Cao Pi has him sent to view the painting of his shameful surrender at Pang De's tomb; dies of shame and grief.
Cao Cao; named in passing in the recap of his death.
Zhuge Liang; in Chengdu; advises Liu Bei to spare Liu Feng - but Liu Bei has already moved to execute.

Cao Pi; deposes Emperor Xian and founds the Wei dynasty. Three rounds of orchestrated 'tianming' theatre - Hua Xin and the court force Emperor Xian to abdicate; Cao Pi 'reluctantly' accepts after the third demand. The Han dynasty formally ends. Cao Pi becomes Emperor Wen of Wei; era name Huangchu year 1 (220 CE in real history).

Hua Xin; the chapter's villain. Drafts the abdication edict; bullies Emperor Xian to the dais; the chief instrument of the Han's burial. Earns the 'unhappy fame' that haunts him in the novel ever after.

Liu Bei; in Chengdu when news of the abdication arrives. Held to be the legitimate Han heir; against his stated wishes is petitioned by his officers (Zhuge Liang, Mi Zhu, Qiao Zhou, Yi Ji, etc.) to declare himself emperor in succession to the Han. Goes into mock-mourning, refuses, refuses again; finally consents. Founds Shu-Han; era Zhangwu. Names Liu Shan heir.
Zhuge Liang; leads the petition for Liu Bei's accession; arranges the auspicious omens and ceremonial; appointed Chancellor (Cheng Xiang). The administrative architect of the new dynasty.
Liu Shan; named heir of Shu-Han at his father's accession. The eventual last emperor.
Chen Qun; in the Wei court; backs the abdication.
Chen Qun (duplicate record).
Cao Xiu; in the Wei court.
Cao Hong; in the Wei court.
Cao Hong (duplicate record).
Huan Jie; in the Wei court; backs the abdication.
Jia Xu; in the Wei court.
Jia Xu (duplicate record).
Sima Yi; in the Wei court; senior official in the new dynasty.
Wang Lang; in the Wei court; backs the abdication.
Wang Lang (duplicate record).
Cao Cao; named in passing - posthumously elevated to Emperor Wu of Wei by Cao Pi after the accession.
Zhao Yun; in Liu Bei's court at the accession.
Zhang Fei; at Langzhong - joins the chorus calling for Liu Bei to take the throne; will lead the Yiling vanguard once Liu Bei consents to march.
Mi Zhu; in Liu Bei's court; among the petitioners for accession.
Qiao Zhou; the chief Shu astrologer-petitioner; produces favourable omens. Will reappear decades later urging surrender to Wei.
Qin Mi; in Liu Bei's court; among the petitioners.
Yi Ji; in Liu Bei's court; among the petitioners.
Xu Jing; in Liu Bei's court; among the petitioners.
Du Qiong; in Liu Bei's court; among the petitioners.
Huang Quan; in Liu Bei's court; among the petitioners. Later defects to Wei during the Yiling retreat.
Meng Guang; in Liu Bei's court; among the petitioners.
Xiang Ju; in Liu Bei's court; among the petitioners.
Yang Hong; in Liu Bei's court; among the petitioners.
Yang Hong (duplicate record).
Yin Chun; in Liu Bei's court.
Yin Mo; in Liu Bei's court.
Yin Mo (duplicate record).
Zhang Shuang; in Liu Bei's court.
Zhang Yin; in Liu Bei's court.
Zhao Zuo; in Liu Bei's court.
Liu Bao; in Liu Bei's court.
Liu Bao (duplicate record).
Liu Bao (duplicate record).
Liu Li; in Liu Bei's court.
Shentu Pan; in Liu Bei's court.
Wang Mou; in Liu Bei's court.
Lady Wu; Liu Bei's consort (sister of Wu Yi); elevated to empress at the accession.
Xu Ci; in Liu Bei's court.
Lai Gong; minor figure named in passing.
He Zong; minor figure named in passing.
Xu Zhi; minor figure named in passing.
Xu Zhi (duplicate record).
Xu Zhi (duplicate record).
Guan Yu; named in passing in the recap of the cause of war.
Sun Quan; off-stage in Wu; receives news of both accessions.
